- **`outdent` drops a node's following siblings instead of re-parenting them under
  it — CONFIRMED, pre-existing, not a Phase C regression.** Direct test: outdenting
  the middle item of `- p\n\t- x\n\t- y\n\t- z\n` (outdenting `x`) produces
  `- p\n\t- y\n\t- z\n\n- x` — `x` jumps to AFTER the entire `p` section (past `y`
  and `z`, its own former following siblings), rather than becoming `p`'s immediate
  next sibling with `y`/`z` re-parented under it. This is the CURRENT, ALREADY-SHIPPED
  behavior of the core `outdent` operation from `mapping-core` (Q2) — no existing
  test in `ops.test.ts`/`closure.test.ts` ever covered "outdenting a node with
  following siblings under the same parent," so the gap shipped unnoticed until this
  change's merge→split→outdent interaction surfaced it in practice (merging a node
  with children into a predecessor, then splitting the predecessor again, then trying
  to outdent the split-off remainder no longer restores the original sibling
  structure — the re-parented children stay with the merged node instead of
  following the split-off node back out). Proposed fix, matching Logseq's outdent
  semantics ("outdent in place"): a node's FOLLOWING siblings (under the same
  parent, after the outdented node) re-parent as the outdented node's OWN children,
  rather than staying with the original parent. This is NOT scoped to
  node-edit-enforcement — it changes core `outdent` behavior for every existing
  scenario with following siblings, well beyond what D10/D11 touch. Holding for an
  explicit decision before implementing (tasks.md 8.2).

input.new_string:
- **`outdent` drops a node's following siblings instead of re-parenting them under
  it — CONFIRMED, pre-existing, not a Phase C regression. FIXED** (change
  `fix-outdent-following-siblings`, 2026-07-23). Direct test: outdenting
  the middle item of `- p\n\t- x\n\t- y\n\t- z\n` (outdenting `x`) used to produce
  `- p\n\t- y\n\t- z\n\n- x` — `x` jumped to AFTER the entire `p` section (past `y`
  and `z`, its own former following siblings), rather than becoming `p`'s immediate
  next sibling with `y`/`z` re-parented under it. This was the CURRENT, ALREADY-SHIPPED
  behavior of the core `outdent` operation from `mapping-core` (Q2) — no existing
  test in `ops.test.ts`/`closure.test.ts` ever covered "outdenting a node with
  following siblings under the same parent," so the gap shipped unnoticed until this
  change's merge→split→outdent interaction surfaced it in practice (merging a node
  with children into a predecessor, then splitting the predecessor again, then trying
  to outdent the split-off remainder no longer restores the original sibling
  structure — the re-parented children stay with the merged node instead of
  following the split-off node back out). Fixed to match Logseq's outdent
  semantics ("outdent in place"): a node's FOLLOWING siblings (under the same
  parent, after the outdented node) now re-parent as the outdented node's OWN
  children (appended after any children it already had), rather than staying with
  the original parent — `outdent(...)` in [src/ops.ts](../../src/ops.ts) now
  truncates `parent`'s remaining children at the outdented node's index and
  re-encodes the removed tail via the same context-determined encoding rule used
  for the outdented node itself. This was NOT scoped to node-edit-enforcement — it
  changed core `outdent` behavior for every existing scenario with following
  siblings, well beyond what D10/D11 touch — see
  `openspec/changes/fix-outdent-following-siblings/` (proposal/design/specs/tasks)
  for the full rationale and regression coverage
  (`tests/ops.test.ts`, `tests/closure.test.ts`).

toolUseResult.originalFile:
## 1. Regression tests (write first, confirm they fail against current `outdent`)

- [x] 1.1 Add `ops.test.ts` case: outdent the middle item of
      `- p\n\t- x\n\t- y\n\t- z\n` (`x`, no pre-existing children) and assert the result is
      `- p\n- x\n\t- y\n\t- z\n` with `y`/`z` as `x`'s children in order.
- [x] 1.2 Add `ops.test.ts` case: outdent a node `x` that already has child `w`, with
      following siblings `y`, `z` — assert `x`'s children become `[w, y, z]` in that order.
- [x] 1.3 Add `ops.test.ts` case: outdent a node with no following siblings (last child of its
      parent) — assert byte-for-byte identical output to current behavior (no regression).
- [x] 1.4 Add `ops.test.ts` case covering a re-parented following sibling whose encoding kind
      changes under its new parent (per `encodingKindAtDestination`), to make the
      context-determined re-encoding visible and intentional.
- [x] 1.5 Add/extend a `closure.test.ts` case exercising `parse(encode(surgery))` round-trip
      for an outdent with following siblings, confirming closure holds.

## 2. Implement the fix

- [x] 2.1 In `src/ops.ts::outdent`, change the `parentPath` surgery from
      `nodes.filter((_, i) => i !== index)` to drop the outdented node AND everything after it
      (`nodes.slice(0, index)`), leaving `parent`'s preceding children untouched.
- [x] 2.2 Compute the re-encoded following siblings (former `nodes.slice(index + 1)`) via
      `reencodeForDestination`/`shiftSubtree`, targeting `moved` as their new parent and
      `childBaseCol(moved)` for their indentation, using
      `encodingKindAtDestination` against `moved`'s own (possibly empty) existing children for
      context, per design.md's encoding decision.
- [x] 2.3 Append the re-encoded following siblings after `moved`'s existing `children` before
      splicing `moved` into `grandPath`.
- [x] 2.4 Carry the trailing gap from the old last child under `parent` onto the new last
      surviving child, or drop it if `parent` now has zero children. **Finding**: no explicit
      carry code was needed — `trailingGap` is owned by the node object itself (not by its
      slot), and following siblings relocate as-is (same objects, same relative left-to-right
      document order, only re-indented/re-parented), so whichever node owned the boundary gap
      before the op still owns it after. Verified by the existing `move()` gap-carry logic
      being a genuinely different case (there, two node identities swap slots, so the
      positional gap must be reassigned) and by the 1500-run `5.1`/`5.2` closure and
      minimal-edit property tests passing unchanged.
- [x] 2.5 Run `renumberOrdered` as needed on both the truncated `parent` children and `moved`'s
      new children list, matching existing ordered-list renumbering behavior.

## 3. Verify

That's the root cause: outline mode applies its own hardcoded default instead of respecting Obsidian's "Use tabs" preference for new indentation. Let me check the `planKey` function signature to confirm.

So `planKey` is a pure function that only receives text, cursor position, and key—it has no access to Obsidian's settings. To honor the "Indent using tabs" preference, I need to pass the user's indent preference down through `indent()` and into `ops.ts`'s `inferIndentUnit` function, which only kicks in when there's no existing indentation pattern to reference. The issue is that the current implementation is forcing spaces regardless of what the user has configured.

Looking at how `indent()` actually works: it calls `destinationIndent()` which tries to match existing sibling indentation first, then falls back to the parent's indent plus whatever `inferIndentUnit()` determines from the document. The function does try to infer from the document's own existing indentation—if any indented list line uses tabs, it uses tabs; otherwise it matches the existing width or defaults to two spaces. So for documents that already have tabs somewhere, new indents correctly use tabs. But for a completely new document with no existing indentation, or the very first indent action, it defaults to spaces regardless of the Obsidian vault setting. That's exactly the bug: the fallback ignores the user's preference.

Now I need to fix this by threading Obsidian's tab preference down through the call chain. content.thinking:
The core issue is that there's no clean way to access the CodeMirror 6 EditorView from the command context in main.ts, since the public API only exposes the `Editor` wrapper. I'm weighing two approaches: either accept the limitation and keep the current spaces-based fallback for these secondary command paths, or find an alternative route to read from `editorInfoField`. The first option seems more pragmatic given the constraints. The practical approach is to compute the indent unit once per keystroke from the CM6 EditorView's facet in the keymap (the common path), while accepting that the command-palette structural commands will fall back to inferring indentation from document content — a reasonable tradeoff since those commands are rare and the inference works fine for existing documents. I could potentially access the CM6 view through `MarkdownView` casting, but given the project's strict API constraints, the current design keeps things clean and documented.

Actually, I can leverage the existing editor extensions infrastructure more elegantly: since my registered extensions like `grammarExtension` and `transactionFilterExtension` already run within the same EditorView, I can add a small ViewPlugin that caches the current indent unit value and exposes it to main.ts through a module-level reference — this stays entirely within public CM6 APIs without any monkey-patching, just tracking state through a legitimate extension. "active view" reference should work reliably since commands always operate on the focused editor, so I can simplify by just storing the current EditorView in a plugin and reading its facet value when needed rather than trying to thread state through multiple layers. Adding optional indent unit parameters... Based on the search results, I found some relevant information about CodeMirror and indentation settings. Here are the key findings:

In CodeMirror 6, the indentUnit can be set to control the number of spaces used for indentation. This involves understanding the facet concept in CodeMirror 6.

The search results also reference an Obsidian plugin for customizing CodeMirror behavior, though the specific details about the "useTab" setting weren't fully detailed in these results.

Regarding Obsidian's native settings, the "Indent using tabs" option can be changed in Obsidian's Editor settings, which controls whether indentation uses tabs or spaces.

The search results are limited in providing the exact technical details about the CodeMirror6 facet configuration with the "useTab" setting you're asking about. For more specific technical documentation about the indentUnit facet and useTab setting in CodeMirror 6, you may need to consult the official CodeMirror 6 documentation or the source code of Obsidian plugins that implement these configurations.

content.content:
Based on my review of the provided GitHub repository content, **the plugin README does not mention the CodeMirror 6 indentUnit facet, useTab setting, or how Obsidian configures tab versus space indentation in its CM6 editor state.**

The document focuses on CodeMirror 5 functionality and features. Notably, it includes this disclaimer:

"This plugin leverages the CM5 API directly which is a deprecated option. Obsidian will be moving to CM6 soon and this plugin will break."

The README was last updated in December 2021, and the repository was archived in August 2023. Since the plugin targets the legacy CodeMirror 5 editor rather than CodeMirror 6, configuration details specific to CM6 facets like indentUnit are not discussed.

For information about Obsidian's CM6 indentation settings, you would need to consult Obsidian's official documentation or the current community plugin ecosystem.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about `getIndentUnit` in the context of CodeMirror and Obsidian plugin development.

The `getIndentUnit` function is imported from `@codemirror/language` and is used in CodeMirror-based editor extensions. This function is used to retrieve the indentation unit (typically the tab size or number of spaces used for indentation) from the editor state.

In the context of an Obsidian plugin, this would be used with the editor's state facet to determine the current indentation settings. The pattern involves accessing `state.facet(indentUnit)` through CodeMirror's state management system to get the indentation configuration for the editor.

Obsidian uses CodeMirror 6 (CM6) to power the Markdown editor, and an Obsidian editor extension is the same thing as a CodeMirror 6 extension, so the CodeMirror API patterns apply directly to Obsidian plugin development.

# Search Results

After searching the provided source code, I found **no instances** of the following terms:
- `indentUnit`
- `getIndentUnit`
- `@codemirror/language`
- `useTab`
- `tabSize`

## How the Plugin Determines Indentation

Based on the code shown, this plugin does not explicitly determine or configure tab/space indentation width in the visible portions. The plugin:

1. Registers editor extensions (`activeIndentField`, `indentationGroup`, `tabDecoration`)
2. Passes settings to `tabDecoration` via a callback: `tabDecoration(() => this.settings)`
3. Manages CSS classes for different indentation contexts (lists, code, uncategorized)

The actual indentation unit/width configuration likely resides in the imported `editorExtensions` module or `Settings` object, which are not included in this excerpt. The plugin appears to rely on these imported modules to handle the specific indentation calculations.

I need to verify whether calling `updateOptions()` alone triggers Obsidian's reconfigure mechanism to refresh the CM6 state and pick up the new `useTab` config value in the `indentUnit` facet. content.thinking:
I'm looking for a test case where the document has no existing indented list items anywhere, so the fallback logic for inferring indentation actually gets triggered, and the pasted content needs to nest as a child of a top-level element without any sibling structure to reference.

Rather than threading this through the whole verdict layer, I'll add unit tests directly in ops.test.ts to verify the fallback behavior of `inferIndentUnit` when handling `insertSubtrees` and related operations, then add one enforce-level test to confirm the plumbing works end-to-end.

The issue is that a single paragraph can't use the no-previous-sibling rejection since there's nothing to indent under. I need at least two top-level paragraphs with no existing list nesting, then indent the second one beneath the first—which triggers the special paragraph handling in the indent function where the destination indent gets calculated using the paragraph's children.

When indenting under a paragraph parent, the function returns the parent's indent directly without invoking inferIndentUnit at all, since paragraph children sit at the same indentation level as the paragraph itself. That's already covered by the "First thought./Second thought." test case. To actually hit the inferIndentUnit fallback, I'd need to indent something under a list-item parent where the document has no other list-items to borrow whitespace from. But if the target is a list-item being indented for the first time with no sibling donors, it goes into the non-paragraph branch and calls inferIndentUnit with a fallback—though this requires the target list-item to be the only one in the document.

Let me trace through a concrete scenario: a document with just "- a\n- b\n" (two top-level list items, completely flat). When I indent "b" under "a", the target becomes "a" with no children yet. The destinationIndent function finds no sibling to donate whitespace, sees that the parent is a list-item, and calculates parentIndent (which is empty since "a" is top-level) plus inferIndentUnit. The inferIndentUnit scan looks at both list-items' leading whitespace—both "- a" and "- b" have empty whitespace since they're at column zero—so the loop finds nothing indented and falls through to return the fallback value.

This matches exactly what my e2e test already covers, so now I should add a unit test directly in ops.test.ts that calls indent with a tab character and verifies the output, plus test the default space behavior without any override.

I'm tracing through the splitNode test case where splitting at character 3 in "- a\n" lands at the end of the line, which means it's an end-of-node split with no remainder, and since the node has no children at that point, it wouldn't trigger the content-adjacent-split branch.

I need to construct a test where the node already has existing children so that splitting within its text creates a remainder that becomes the new first child ahead of those existing children. The tricky part is making sure the destination indent calculation doesn't get confused by the existing children when determining what kind of indent unit to use.

The cleanest approach is to create a list item with a nested paragraph child — that way when checking for list-item siblings to donate indentation, the paragraph child won't match, forcing the fallback logic to kick in and infer the indent unit from the parent's context. I'm testing whether a markdown structure like a list item containing a paragraph can properly handle the split and remainder placement.

content.thinking:
I'm working through how the child kind gets determined when splitting at a specific position—checking the preceding and following siblings to find a donor element that establishes what kind of node should be created, and in this case the paragraph from the following siblings becomes the donor.

The issue is that when I have an empty remainder with a paragraph child kind, the code falls through to a special case that treats it like a childless sibling instead of using the destination indent logic. To properly test the fallback behavior, I need to construct a scenario where the child kind is a list-item (not paragraph) so the fallthrough doesn't trigger, while also ensuring the node's children aren't list-items themselves—that way the destination indent logic actually gets exercised.

But there's a catch: if the node has children at all, and any of them is a list-item, that list-item becomes the donor and its indentation gets copied directly, which bypasses the fallback I'm trying to test. And if the node has no children, the whole content-adjacent split branch doesn't even execute. So I'm stuck—I can't simultaneously have a list-item child kind with a non-empty remainder AND avoid having a list-item donor that would copy indentation directly. So the fallback parameter gets exercised exactly in this scenario. Let me verify this with a test case where I split a list-item with an existing paragraph child at a non-empty remainder position—say at character 2, right after the "- " marker, which leaves "a" as the remainder.

The fallback parameter is working as expected—when I use it, the new first-child paragraph gets indented with a tab while the original child paragraph retains its 2-space indentation. Now I need to test the behavior without the fallback to confirm it defaults to 2 spaces, though I'll need a fresh document for the second test since the first one was consumed.

content.thinking:
The issue is that `a` gets its id from one parsed document, but when I call `splitNode` with a freshly parsed tree, those node ids don't match anymore since each parse generates new incrementing ids. I need to either find the node by its line number in each document separately, or just reuse the same parsed document instance throughout.

Actually, since `splitNode` is a pure function that returns a new immutable document without mutating the original, I can safely reuse the same `doc` and `a` reference across both calls — the original stays untouched.

input.old_string: const withoutFallback = splitNode(parse(src), a, { line: 0, ch: 2 });
input.new_string: const withoutFallback = splitNode(doc, a, { line: 0, ch: 2 });

- **THEN** those children keep their order and relative structure under the merged
  node, re-encoded for the new scope, and the result re-parses to exactly that tree

#### Scenario: Single-line content joins a heading
- **WHEN** `mergeNodes` joins a heading with a following single-line paragraph
- **THEN** the paragraph's text appends to the heading's title line; a multi-line
  paragraph in the same position is rejected with `merge-not-expressible`

### Requirement: Subtree insertion at a boundary
An `insertSubtrees` operation SHALL splice a parsed sequence of whole subtrees into
the tree at a node boundary (before or after an anchor node), re-encoded at a depth
valid for the anchor's scope per the mapping algebra (heading levels bounded,
list/paragraph depth encodings converted as the existing reparenting rules require).
Sequences inexpressible at the target scope SHALL be rejected rather than inserted
in corrupted form. When no kind conversion is needed (the common case — the
sequence's own top-level kind already matches the destination context), each
subtree's original indent characters SHALL carry through verbatim beyond its own
top-level prefix, re-rooted at the destination depth — not expressed as a flat
numeric width delta, which can introduce a mismatched indent unit (e.g. spaces
inserted into an otherwise all-tab subtree) at any depth beyond the first level.

#### Scenario: List items pasted under a deeper scope re-indent
- **WHEN** `insertSubtrees` places two top-level list-item subtrees after a list item
  nested two levels deep
- **THEN** the inserted items are re-encoded at the anchor's depth with their
  internal relative structure preserved

#### Scenario: A single node's nested children keep a consistent indent unit at any target depth
- **WHEN** `insertSubtrees` places ONE top-level list-item subtree — itself with a
  child two levels deep, all tab-indented — after an anchor at a depth different
  from where the subtree was originally encoded
- **THEN** every line in the inserted subtree, at every depth, uses the SAME indent
  character the anchor's own context uses — no mix of the original tabs with
  newly-added spaces at any level

#### Scenario: Insertion never splices mid-node
- **WHEN** `insertSubtrees` is invoked with any anchor
- **THEN** every existing node's own lines remain contiguous and byte-identical —
  inserted content only ever lands between nodes

input.content:
## Why

Outline mode always materializes brand-new list indentation as two literal spaces,
ignoring Obsidian's own "Indent using tabs" editor setting (Settings → Editor). Any
vault configured for tabs (or a different space width) gets mixed indentation the
moment the outliner creates a fresh indent level with nothing in the document yet to
infer a unit from — the first Tab in a new note, the first structural paste into an
otherwise-flat scope, an Enter-driven split that materializes a new nested child.
Since `structural-operations`' own "Context-determined encoding on reparent" rule
already infers the unit from EXISTING document content when there is any, this only
bites the "nothing to infer from yet" case — but that case is common (every new note,
every first indent) and the mismatch it produces is exactly the kind of "breaks
indentation in multiple cases" a user notices immediately.

## What Changes

- `ops.ts`'s `inferIndentUnit`/`destinationIndent` gain an optional
  `fallbackIndentUnit` parameter, used only when the document itself has no existing
  indented list item to infer a unit from (unchanged default: two spaces, so every
  existing pure-function caller/test is unaffected).
- This threads through `indent`, `outdent`, `splitNode`, `reencodeBlocksForDestination`,
  `insertSubtrees` (ops.ts) and `computeVerdict` and its internal helpers (enforce.ts),
  covering every place brand-new indentation can be materialized: Tab/Shift-Tab,
  Enter-driven splits, and structural paste/type-over/merge-splice rewrites.
- The CM6 adapters (`keymap.ts` for Tab/Shift-Tab, `transaction-filter.ts` for the
  paste/type-over verdict path) supply the live value by reading the PUBLIC
  `@codemirror/language` `indentUnit` facet off the editor state — confirmed live
  against a real Obsidian instance that this facet tracks Obsidian's "Indent using
  tabs" setting exactly, with no private `vault.getConfig`/`vault.setConfig` API
  needed in the shipped plugin code (this project's architecture bar: 100% public
  Obsidian APIs).
- **Known gap, not fixed here**: `main.ts`'s command-palette structural commands
  (`indent-node`/`outdent-node`) have no public-API path to the live CM6 facet from
  Obsidian's `Editor`/`MarkdownView` types, so they keep inferring from document
  content only (same as before this change) — documented inline at the `StructuralOp`
  type. This is a narrow, secondary entry point; Tab/Shift-Tab and paste (the
  everyday paths) are fully fixed.
- New devDependency: `@codemirror/language` (types only — already externalized in
  `esbuild.config.mjs`, resolved to Obsidian's own bundled copy at runtime, so no
  extra bytes ship and the facet reference is the SAME instance Obsidian itself sets).

## Capabilities

### New Capabilities
(none)

### Modified Capabilities
- `structural-operations`: brand-new indentation (no existing document evidence to
  infer a unit from) now takes the vault's "Indent using tabs" preference instead of
  a hardcoded two-space default.

## Impact

- `src/ops.ts`, `src/enforce.ts`: optional trailing parameter threaded through the
  functions listed above; no behavior change for any existing caller that omits it.
- `src/plugin/keymap.ts`, `src/plugin/transaction-filter.ts`: read the public CM6
  `indentUnit` facet and pass it through.
- `src/plugin/main.ts`: doc comment only, no behavior change (documents the known
  command-palette gap).
- `src/plugin/grammar.ts`: `planKey` gains the same optional parameter.
- `package.json`/`package-lock.json`: `@codemirror/language` devDependency added.
- Tests: new unit coverage in `tests/ops.test.ts` (fallback-unit behavior for
  `indent`/`splitNode`), a new e2e test in `e2e/specs/30-keyboard-grammar.e2e.ts`
  toggling the real Obsidian setting and asserting Tab output, and a new
  test-setup-only `setIndentUsingTabs` helper in `e2e/helpers.ts`.

input.content:
## Context

Outline mode's structural ops (`ops.ts`) are pure functions with no Obsidian imports
by design — `document-tree-mapping`/`structural-operations` keep the mapping algebra
testable without a live editor. When a node needs brand-new indentation and the
document offers no existing indented list item to infer a unit from,
`destinationIndent`'s `inferIndentUnit` fell back to a hardcoded `'  '` (two spaces),
regardless of Obsidian's own "Indent using tabs" vault setting. This project's
architecture bar (docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md) is "100% public
Obsidian APIs, no private APIs, no monkey-patching" — `Vault.getConfig`/`setConfig`
(the common but undocumented way plugins read this setting) is not part of the public
`obsidian.d.ts` surface, so the fix needed a public-API route to the live setting.

## Goals / Non-Goals

**Goals:**
- Brand-new indentation (no existing document evidence) uses the vault's configured
  tab/space preference, live, without any Obsidian-private API in shipped plugin code.
- Zero behavior change for every existing pure-function call site that doesn't pass
  the new parameter (all existing tests keep passing unmodified).
- Cover both everyday paths that can materialize new indentation: Tab/Shift-Tab
  (`keymap.ts`) and structural paste/type-over/merge-splice rewrites
  (`transaction-filter.ts` → `enforce.ts`).

**Non-Goals:**
- Fixing `main.ts`'s command-palette structural commands — no public API exposes the
  live CM6 facet from `Editor`/`MarkdownView`; documented as a known, narrow gap
  rather than worked around with a private accessor.
- Changing how an EXISTING document's own indentation is inferred (list-item-donor
  and existing-tab/space detection are unchanged and still take priority — this only
  changes the last-resort fallback).
- Any change to `indentUnit`'s own semantics or CodeMirror's indentation service —
  this only READS the facet, never sets it.

## Decisions

### Read Obsidian's setting via CM6's public `indentUnit` facet, not `vault.getConfig`
`@codemirror/language` exports `indentUnit: Facet<string, string>` — "a string
consisting entirely of the same whitespace character... when not set, defaults to 2
spaces." Verified empirically against a real Obsidian instance (via the e2e harness's
`executeObsidian`, probing `view.editor.cm.state.facet(indentUnit)` before/after
toggling `useTab`): Obsidian sets this facet from its own "Indent using tabs" setting,
live — `useTab: false` → `"    "` (spaces, width = the configured `tabSize`);
`useTab: true` → `"\t"`. Reading `EditorView.state.facet(indentUnit)` (keymap.ts) or
`Transaction.startState.facet(indentUnit)` (transaction-filter.ts) is plain public CM6
API — no Obsidian-internal call at all.
**Alternative considered**: `(app.vault as any).getConfig('useTab')` / `'tabSize'` —
works, is what most community plugins do, but is explicitly the kind of undocumented
internal this project's architecture bar rules out; also would need a second call for
`tabSize` and its own space-string construction, duplicating what the facet already
gives as one ready-to-use string.
**Alternative considered**: track a `ViewPlugin`-based "active editor" reference on
the plugin instance so `main.ts`'s commands could also read the facet — would close
the command-palette gap, but adds a standing piece of mutable cross-context state for
a narrow, secondary entry point; deferred rather than adding that surface now.

### Thread as an optional trailing parameter, not a required one
Every function in the `ops.ts`/`enforce.ts` chain that can materialize new
indentation (`indent`, `outdent`, `splitNode`, `reencodeBlocksForDestination`,
`insertSubtrees`, `computeVerdict` and its private helpers) gains
`fallbackIndentUnit?: string` as its last parameter, defaulting through to the
existing two-space behavior when omitted.
**Alternative considered**: an options object (`{fallbackIndentUnit}`) — more
extensible if more editor-derived preferences show up later, but every one of these
functions already has 2-5 positional parameters and the existing call sites/tests are
positional; a single optional trailing string keeps the diff minimal and every
existing call site untouched.
**Alternative considered**: a module-level "current preference" singleton set by the
plugin on load/setting-change — rejected, breaks the pure-function/no-Obsidian-import
design `ops.ts`/`enforce.ts` deliberately keep (design.md's own stated goal for
testability), and goes stale the instant the user flips the setting without a reload.

### Only the FALLBACK changes — existing-document inference still wins
`destinationIndent`'s priority order is unchanged: (1) an existing list-item sibling
at the destination donates its indentation verbatim, (2) else infer a unit from any
indented list item anywhere in the document, (3) else — now — the caller-supplied
fallback, still defaulting to two spaces. A vault-wide "use tabs" preference does NOT
retroactively change an already-tab-or-space-committed document's own established
style; it only governs indentation with zero existing evidence, matching the existing
"tab-indented vaults... adopt the destination level indentation" design intent
(structural-operations spec) rather than overriding it.

## Risks / Trade-offs

- **[Risk] `@codemirror/language` version drift between this plugin's devDependency
  and Obsidian's actual bundled version** → Mitigation: the package is in
  `esbuild.config.mjs`'s existing `external` list (already present before this
  change, for `@codemirror/state`/`@codemirror/view` reasons) — it is NEVER bundled,
  only used for TypeScript types at build time; at runtime Obsidian's module resolver
  supplies its own instance, so the `indentUnit` Facet object identity is always
  Obsidian's own, regardless of the devDependency's pinned version. Facet APIs this
  stable (2 spaces default, string-based unit) are very unlikely to break across CM6
  6.x versions regardless.
- **[Risk] Command-palette structural commands stay inconsistent with Tab/Shift-Tab**
  → Mitigation: documented inline (main.ts's `StructuralOp` comment) and in this
  proposal's Non-Goals; the everyday paths are fixed, and this is a pre-existing
  inconsistency this change doesn't worsen.
- **[Risk] `e2e/helpers.ts`'s new `setIndentUsingTabs` uses `vault.setConfig`, the
  same private API the shipped plugin code avoids** → Mitigation: this is test-setup
  scaffolding arranging Obsidian's own state for a test, not plugin runtime code —
  same category as this file's pre-existing `(editor as any).cm` reads used
  elsewhere in the harness; the architecture bar applies to what ships in `main.js`.

## Migration Plan

Pure addition of optional parameters plus two read-only facet reads — no data
migration, no schema change, no settings UI added (the plugin doesn't introduce a new
setting; it respects Obsidian's existing one). Rollback is a plain revert. Verified via:
1. Unit tests (`tests/ops.test.ts`) pinning both the unchanged default and the
   fallback-supplied behavior, plus that existing-document inference still wins over
   the fallback.
2. A real-Obsidian e2e test (`e2e/specs/30-keyboard-grammar.e2e.ts`) toggling the
   actual "Indent using tabs" setting and asserting Tab produces the corresponding
   character — the empirical proof, not just a mocked facet read.
3. Full existing unit (816) and e2e (130+, 12 spec files) suites re-run clean.

## Open Questions

- Whether to later close the command-palette gap via a tracked-active-view extension
  (see Decisions, alternative considered) — not needed unless a user reports the
  inconsistency in practice.

## Context

by design — `document-tree-mapping`/`structural-operations` keep the mapping algebra
testable without a live editor. When a node needs brand-new indentation and the
document offers no existing indented list item to infer a unit from,
`destinationIndent`'s `inferIndentUnit` fell back to a hardcoded `'  '` (two spaces),
regardless of Obsidian's own "Indent using tabs" vault setting. This project's
architecture bar (docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md) is "100% public
Obsidian APIs, no private APIs, no monkey-patching" — `Vault.getConfig`/`setConfig`
(the common but undocumented way plugins read this setting) is not part of the public
`obsidian.d.ts` surface, so the fix needed a public-API route to the live setting.

## Goals / Non-Goals

**Goals:**
- Brand-new indentation (no existing document evidence) uses the vault's configured
  tab/space preference, live, without any Obsidian-private API in shipped plugin code.
- Zero behavior change for every existing pure-function call site that doesn't pass
  the new parameter (all existing tests keep passing unmodified).
- Cover both everyday paths that can materialize new indentation: Tab/Shift-Tab
  (`keymap.ts`) and structural paste/type-over/merge-splice rewrites

**Non-Goals:**

  live CM6 facet from `Editor`/`MarkdownView`; documented as a known, narrow gap
  rather than worked around with a private accessor.
- Changing how an EXISTING document's own indentation is inferred (list-item-donor
  and existing-tab/space detection are unchanged and still take priority — this only
  changes the last-resort fallback).

  this only READS the facet, never sets it.

content.content:
### Requirement: Context-determined encoding on reparent (provisional rule)
A reparented non-heading node's markdown encoding SHALL be recomputed as a pure function of
its new surroundings: it takes the block type of its nearest preceding sibling under the new
parent; if none, the following sibling; if it has no siblings, it encodes as a paragraph
under a heading or the root, and as a list item under any other parent. This rule SHALL be
implemented behind an isolated strategy function.

#### Scenario: Indent then outdent restores a paragraph
- **WHEN** a top-level paragraph is indented under a paragraph and then outdented back
- **THEN** it is re-encoded as a paragraph (nearest sibling at the destination is a
  paragraph) and the document is byte-identical to the original

#### Scenario: Nested-list documents never flatten
- **WHEN** outdent is applied to any item in a document consisting entirely of nested list
  items
- **THEN** the item remains a list item at its new depth (all destination siblings are list
  items)

### Requirement: Sibling reordering

input.content:
## ADDED Requirements

### Requirement: Fallback indent unit for brand-new indentation
When a structural operation must materialize indentation with no existing evidence in
the document to infer a unit from (no destination-sibling list item to copy
whitespace from, and no other indented list item anywhere in the document —
`destinationIndent`'s existing-document-inference steps both come up empty), the
operation SHALL accept an optional caller-supplied fallback indent unit and use it
instead of an unconditional default. When no fallback is supplied, the existing
two-space default SHALL still apply, so this is purely additive: no existing behavior
changes unless a caller opts in. Existing-document inference SHALL still take priority
over the fallback whenever it has evidence to act on — the fallback only ever governs
the true no-evidence case.

#### Scenario: No fallback supplied keeps the existing two-space default
- **WHEN** a node is indented under a list-item parent with no existing indented list
  item anywhere in the document, and no fallback indent unit is supplied
- **THEN** the new indentation is two spaces, exactly as before this requirement existed

#### Scenario: A supplied fallback governs brand-new indentation
- **WHEN** the same indent is performed with a caller-supplied fallback of a tab
  character (or a specific space width)
- **THEN** the new indentation uses that exact unit instead of the two-space default

#### Scenario: Existing document indentation still wins over the fallback
- **WHEN** the document already has an indented list item using tabs elsewhere, and a
  node is indented under a list-item parent with no fallback OR a spaces-based
  fallback supplied
- **THEN** the new indentation still infers tabs from the existing document content —
  the fallback never overrides an already-established indentation style

content.text: Let's save a memory about the key non-obvious finding — this is exactly the kind of reusable, hard-won discovery worth remembering.

input.content:
---
name: obsidian-indent-unit-facet
description: How to read Obsidian's "Indent using tabs" setting from a plugin using only public APIs
metadata:
  type: project
---

Obsidian's "Indent using tabs" editor setting (Settings → Editor) is readable from
plugin code as the PUBLIC CodeMirror 6 `indentUnit` facet
(`import { indentUnit } from '@codemirror/language'`), via
`editorView.state.facet(indentUnit)` or `transaction.startState.facet(indentUnit)` —
NOT via the private/undocumented `app.vault.getConfig('useTab')` most community
plugins use.

**Why:** confirmed empirically against a real Obsidian instance (via
wdio-obsidian-service's `executeObsidian`, in
[obsidian-true-outliner](../../../Code/obsidian-true-outliner)): toggling `useTab`
and calling `app.workspace.updateOptions()` changes `state.facet(indentUnit)` live —
`useTab: false` → a space string (width = `tabSize`), `useTab: true` → `"\t"`.
`@codemirror/language` must be in the esbuild `external` list (never bundled) so the
facet reference resolves to Obsidian's own module instance at runtime, not a
separately-bundled copy — facets rely on reference identity, so a bundled copy
would silently never match. This project's architecture bar is "100% public Obsidian
APIs, no private APIs" (see docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md), so this
facet route was the correct fix for obsidian-true-outliner's outline mode hardcoding
2-space indentation regardless of the vault's tab/space preference
(`fix: respect-tab-space-indent-setting` change).

**How to apply:** whenever a future Obsidian plugin task needs to read editor-level
preferences (tab/space, tab width, etc.) that CodeMirror itself models as a facet,
check `@codemirror/language`/`@codemirror/state` for the relevant public facet before

toolUseResult.content:
---
name: obsidian-indent-unit-facet
description: "How to read Obsidian's \"Indent using tabs\" setting from a plugin using only public APIs"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 187ac644-579c-445e-b4e5-d7f150478370
  modified: 2026-07-23T01:04:02.869Z
---

Obsidian's "Indent using tabs" editor setting (Settings → Editor) is readable from
plugin code as the PUBLIC CodeMirror 6 `indentUnit` facet
(`import { indentUnit } from '@codemirror/language'`), via
`editorView.state.facet(indentUnit)` or `transaction.startState.facet(indentUnit)` —
NOT via the private/undocumented `app.vault.getConfig('useTab')` most community
plugins use.

**Why:** confirmed empirically against a real Obsidian instance (via
wdio-obsidian-service's `executeObsidian`, in
[obsidian-true-outliner](../../../Code/obsidian-true-outliner)): toggling `useTab`
and calling `app.workspace.updateOptions()` changes `state.facet(indentUnit)` live —
`useTab: false` → a space string (width = `tabSize`), `useTab: true` → `"\t"`.
`@codemirror/language` must be in the esbuild `external` list (never bundled) so the
facet reference resolves to Obsidian's own module instance at runtime, not a
separately-bundled copy — facets rely on reference identity, so a bundled copy
would silently never match. This project's architecture bar is "100% public Obsidian
APIs, no private APIs" (see docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md), so this
facet route was the correct fix for obsidian-true-outliner's outline mode hardcoding
2-space indentation regardless of the vault's tab/space preference
(`fix: respect-tab-space-indent-setting` change).

**How to apply:** whenever a future Obsidian plugin task needs to read editor-level
preferences (tab/space, tab width, etc.) that CodeMirror itself models as a facet,
check `@codemirror/language`/`@codemirror/state` for the relevant public facet before
reaching for `vault.getConfig`. Only entry points with a real `EditorView`/
`EditorState` in scope can do this (CM6 keymap handlers, transaction filters) — a
plugin's `Editor`/`MarkdownView` abstraction (used by command-palette callbacks) does
NOT publicly expose the underlying CM6 state, so that path has no public-API route to
the same facet (a documented, accepted gap in the same change, left to command-palette
users as a known inconsistency with Tab-key/paste behavior).

content.text:
## Summary

Investigated and fixed the tab/space indentation bug.

**Root cause:** `ops.ts`'s `inferIndentUnit` hardcoded `'  '` (two spaces) as the fallback whenever a structural op had to materialize brand-new indentation with nothing in the document yet to infer a unit from (first Tab in a new note, structural paste into a flat scope, etc.) — completely ignoring Obsidian's "Indent using tabs" setting.

**The fix respects the setting via a fully public API**, not the private `vault.getConfig` most plugins use: CodeMirror 6's own `indentUnit` facet (`@codemirror/language`), which I confirmed empirically against a real Obsidian instance tracks the setting live. This matches the project's "100% public APIs, no private APIs" bar.

**Changes:**
- [src/ops.ts](src/ops.ts) / [src/enforce.ts](src/enforce.ts): threaded an optional `fallbackIndentUnit` parameter through every function that can materialize new indentation — fully backward-compatible (all 816 existing tests pass unchanged).
- [src/plugin/keymap.ts](src/plugin/keymap.ts) (Tab/Shift-Tab) and [src/plugin/transaction-filter.ts](src/plugin/transaction-filter.ts) (paste/type-over/merge-splice) now read the live `indentUnit` facet and pass it through — the two everyday paths that create new indentation.
- **Known gap, documented not fixed**: [src/plugin/main.ts](src/plugin/main.ts)'s command-palette `indent-node`/`outdent-node` commands have no public API to reach the live facet from Obsidian's `Editor` type, so they keep the old document-inference-only behavior.
- Added `@codemirror/language` as a devDependency (types only — already externalized in the build, resolves to Obsidian's own instance at runtime).
- New unit tests ([tests/ops.test.ts](tests/ops.test.ts)) and a real-Obsidian e2e test ([e2e/specs/30-keyboard-grammar.e2e.ts](e2e/specs/30-keyboard-grammar.e2e.ts)) that toggles the actual setting and asserts Tab produces a literal tab vs. spaces. Full e2e suite (130+ tests, 12 files) passes.

Documented as a new OpenSpec change: [`respect-tab-space-indent-setting`](openspec/changes/respect-tab-space-indent-setting/) (proposal/design/specs/tasks, implementation complete, only spec-sync left). Both this fix and the earlier outdent fix are on the `fix-outdent-following-siblings` branch, uncommitted — let me know if you'd like me to commit.

cd /Users/laughedelic/Code/obsidian-true-outliner
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(ops): outdent re-parents following siblings instead of dropping them

outdent() moved only the target node to uncle level, leaving its former
following siblings behind under the old parent instead of following it
out — a pre-existing gap in the core outdent algebra (mapping-core),
never covered by ops.test.ts/closure.test.ts, that outline-edit-
enforcement's merge->split->outdent interaction surfaced in practice
(docs/research/04-open-questions.md Q17).

Fixed to match Logseq's outdent-in-place semantics: a node's following
siblings under the same former parent now re-parent as its own trailing
children (appended after any children it already had), re-encoded via
the same context-determined encoding rule already used for the
outdented node itself. Regression coverage in ops.test.ts and
closure.test.ts for the exact Q17 repro plus pre-existing-children,
no-following-siblings, and re-encoding-on-reparent variants.

See openspec/changes/fix-outdent-following-siblings/ for the full
proposal/design/specs.
EOF
)"
git log --oneline -3

attachment.snippet:
1	# Open Questions & Decisions
2	
3	Alignment log for pre-planning decisions. ✅ = decided, ❓ = open.
4	Three alignment rounds on 2026-07-12; all pre-planning questions are now decided except Q10
5	(explicitly post-v1).
6	
7	## Q1. Architecture path ✅ DECIDED: editor-centric (Option A/C)
8	
9	CM6 extensions in the standard markdown view + our own side panes. 100% public API.
10	See [03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md](03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md).
11	
12	## Q2. Scope of "outline mode" ✅ DECIDED: universal isomorphic outline view
13	
14	Neither per-file opt-in nor vault-wide enforcement. The owner's framing (near-verbatim):
15	
16	> The outliner experience is all about the **editor UI, not the content under it**. Any "flat"
17	> markdown note actually has structure: a hierarchy implied by headings of different levels,
18	> each paragraph is a separate node, lists are natural outliner subtrees. Any existing markdown
19	> note can be transformed into an outline by mapping that hierarchical structure onto the tree,
20	> providing the same editing experience as for a nested bullet-list outline. A UI toggle turns
21	> any note into an outline (no change for list-only content), and back. **This has to be an
22	> isomorphic transformation.**
23	
24	Implications:
25	
26	- The document model is the **full markdown block tree**: heading nodes (level = depth
27	  anchor), block nodes (paragraph, code fence, quote, table, …), and list-item nodes —
28	  not just lists. Obsidian's `CachedMetadata` (`sections` + `headings` + `listItems`) already
29	  parses exactly this tree — see the feasibility addendum.
30	- The toggle is a **view/UI mode change, never a content rewrite**. For a pure-list note the
31	  outline view is visually identical to today's list editing.
32	- **Isomorphism = lossless round-trip**: md → tree → md must be identity; every structural
33	  operation in outline mode must produce a tree that maps back to valid, natural markdown.
34	- "Both without compromises": flat-markdown notes keep full outliner features *through the
35	  mapping*, instead of losing them (obsidian-outliner's model) or being forced into bullets
36	  (Logseq's model).
37	
38	### Q2 follow-ups (the mapping algebra) ✅ DECIDED (2026-07-12, second alignment)
39	
40	**The unifying principle** (governs all structural ops):
41	
42	> Every structural op either writes the **minimal markdown encoding of the new tree**, or —
43	> when no encoding exists — is **rejected with gentle feedback**. Never hidden state, never
44	> lossy conversion.
45	
46	**The two-regime algebra** (third alignment refinement):
47	
48	```
49	HEADINGS         Tab/S-Tab = level ± 1 (org-mode promote/demote), whole
50	                 subtree shifts; the tree re-derives from levels; reject
51	                 only at the h1/h6 bounds.
52	EVERYTHING ELSE  Tab/S-Tab = reparent (child-of-previous-sibling /
53	                 brother→uncle); encoding recomputed from the new context.
54	ALWAYS           minimal encoding or reject; no hidden state.
55	```
56	
57	1. **Paragraph under paragraph** ✅ *provisional*: a list following a paragraph is that
58	   paragraph's **children** in outline mode. So indenting paragraph B under paragraph A turns
59	   B into a list item after A; A stays an intact paragraph. Top-level paragraphs are never
60	   auto-converted — a flat document just becomes a long flat list of nodes when toggled.
61	   *Marked provisional*: revisit after the first prototype; may become configurable.
62	   *Alternatives considered and rejected*: (a) lists attach only to headings, never
63	   paragraphs — makes paragraph-with-children inexpressible, killing the indent-under-
64	   paragraph op entirely; (b) sentinel syntax (e.g. a paragraph ending in `:` claims the
65	   following list) — magic content-sniffing, fails isomorphism. Adjacency-as-parenthood is
66	   the only lossless way to give paragraphs children; the heading/paragraph asymmetry is
67	   markdown's (headings *scope* what follows; paragraphs merely *precede* it).
68	2. **Heading nodes** ✅: Tab/Shift+Tab = **level ± 1** (org-mode promote/demote semantics),
69	   the whole subtree's headings shift with it (marker-only edits; `[[note#Heading]]` anchors
70	   are text-based and survive). Rejected only at the bounds (no h0, no h7). **Skipped levels**
71	   (h1 → h3) are preserved; tree depth = tree position, not raw level; outdenting a
72	   skip-leveled heading first normalizes the level (### → ## under an h1: level changes,
73	   hierarchy doesn't), then the next outdent changes hierarchy — and symmetrically, Tab on a
74	   heading may create a skip (a "styling-only" edit where the node's tree position is
75	   unchanged and only the visible level marker deepens). Accepted consequence, same as org.
76	3. **Context-determined encoding on reparent** ✅ *provisional*: a reparented node's encoding
77	   is a pure function of its new surroundings — it takes the type of its nearest preceding
78	   sibling under the new parent (fallback: following sibling; no siblings: paragraph under a
79	   heading/root, list item under anything else). Consequences: paragraph → indent → outdent
80	   round-trips back to a paragraph (new sibling is a paragraph), while outdenting inside a
81	   100%-nested-list document keeps everything list items (all neighbors are list items) — no
82	   flattening, and no hidden per-node memory of original types.
83	4. **Mixed containment** ✅: a list item can never contain a heading — under the two-regime
84	   algebra no op can even attempt it (headings move by level, not by reparenting), so this
85	   holds by construction rather than by rejection.
86	5. **Leaf-only blocks** ✅: code fences, tables, callouts, quotes are movable/indentable
87	   **atoms**; internal content is not outline-structured.
88	6. **Toggle persistence** ✅: remembered per note in the **plugin data store** (consistent
89	   with Q3 — files stay clean; frontmatter would pollute content for a pure UI mode).
90	
91	See [05-org-mode-comparison.md](05-org-mode-comparison.md) for where this algebra aligns
92	with and diverges from org-mode, the closest living reference system.
93	
94	### Verdicts from the mapping-core implementation (2026-07-12, `mapping-core` change)
95	
96	Both provisional rules **held up** under property testing (byte-identity round-trip,
97	op-closure, inverse laws — thousands of generated cases). Findings to carry forward:
98	
99	- **Attachment rule: KEEP.** Confirmed consequence: "list item as the sibling directly
100	  after a paragraph" is unrepresentable — the tree generator itself had to fold such
101	  shapes into children, which is the rule working as designed. Scope refinement
102	  discovered: in v1 the rule applies at *section level* (root/heading children); inside
103	  a list item's children, a paragraph and a following list parse as siblings under the
104	  item. Revisit whether nested paragraphs should also capture lists.
105	- **Context-determined encoding: KEEP.** Delivered exactly the promised laws: paragraph
106	  indent∘outdent restores the document byte-identically, and pure-list documents never
107	  flatten. The donor scan considers only paragraph/list-item siblings (headings/atoms
108	  are skipped) — heading nodes are never produced by re-encoding.
109	- **New rejection discovered — outdent out of a heading section.** Heading scope is
110	  positional in markdown: content placed "after the section" is still *in* the section,
111	  so brother→uncle for a direct child of a heading has no encoding → rejected
112	  (`not-expressible-under-target`). UX implication for the CM6 layer: outdent at
113	  section level needs affordance messaging (or a future "split section" op).

input.command:
cd /Users/laughedelic/Code/obsidian-true-outliner
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(indent): respect Obsidian's "Indent using tabs" setting for brand-new indentation

Outline mode always materialized brand-new list indentation as two
literal spaces, ignoring Obsidian's own "Indent using tabs" editor
setting. This only bites when a structural op has no existing document
evidence to infer a unit from (the first Tab in a new note, a
structural paste/type-over into an otherwise-flat scope, an
Enter-driven split materializing a new nested child) -- but that case
is common and the mismatch it produces is immediately visible.

Threaded an optional fallbackIndentUnit parameter through ops.ts
(indent/outdent/splitNode/reencodeBlocksForDestination/insertSubtrees)
and enforce.ts's verdict chain, used only when the document itself has
no existing indented list item to infer from (existing-document
inference still takes priority; default stays two spaces when omitted,
so every existing caller/test is unaffected).

The CM6 adapters (keymap.ts for Tab/Shift-Tab, transaction-filter.ts
for the paste/type-over/merge-splice verdict path) supply the live
value by reading the PUBLIC @codemirror/language `indentUnit` facet --
confirmed empirically against a real Obsidian instance that this facet
tracks "Indent using tabs" exactly, with no private vault.getConfig/
setConfig API needed in shipped plugin code (this project's bar: 100%
public Obsidian APIs). main.ts's command-palette structural commands
have no public-API path to the live facet from Editor/MarkdownView, so
they keep the prior document-inference-only behavior -- documented
inline as a known, narrow gap.

New devDependency: @codemirror/language (types only -- already
externalized in esbuild.config.mjs, resolves to Obsidian's own bundled
copy at runtime).

New unit coverage in ops.test.ts and a real-Obsidian e2e test in
30-keyboard-grammar.e2e.ts that toggles the actual setting and asserts
Tab output for both tab and space configurations.

See openspec/changes/respect-tab-space-indent-setting/ for the full
proposal/design/specs.
EOF
)"
git log --oneline -3

content.thinking:
So the issue is actually in the `mergeNodes` function in ops.ts, not in the functions I've already patched — this handles the Backspace operation that merges a list item into a paragraph. I need to look at how `mergeNodes` recalculates indentation for child nodes when performing the merge, specifically the logic that computes the difference between the base column positions of the two nodes being merged. Examining the shiftLine function logic...

When processing "\t- child1" with a -2 shift, the loop removes 4 width units for the tab character, leaving remaining at -2, then prepends 2 spaces to compensate. This produces "  - child1" instead of properly shifting the tab, which matches the broken output exactly—the numeric-width approach converts tabs into spaces, corrupting the vault's tab-based indentation structure, the same risky pattern that mergeNodes relies on despite the documented warnings. The mixed whitespace indentation breaks the parser's list-item detection since it expects consistent indentation, treating the malformed line as a paragraph continuation instead of a nested list item. The root cause is that `mergeNodes`'s `shiftSubtree` function uses raw column-counting for dedentation without preserving the original tab-versus-space character composition, so when it calculates remainders it converts them to spaces, creating this inconsistent indentation that confuses the parser.

This isn't actually about the "Indent using tabs" setting itself — it's about preserving whatever indentation character the subtree already uses when reparenting it, similar to how `insertSubtrees` was fixed with `reindentSubtreeVerbatim`. I need to check whether this is a pre-existing bug or something my change introduced by looking at whether `mergeNodes` calls `destinationIndent` or `inferIndentUnit` at all, since I confirmed earlier that it currently uses `shiftSubtree` directly without those functions.

The repro bug appears to be a separate, pre-existing issue in `mergeNodes`'s child-reindentation logic — it shifts numerically rather than preserving the literal indentation characters. But the user's comment that the change "isn't complete yet" suggests they think this repro demonstrates that my current fix doesn't fully address the tab/space problem, so I should reconsider whether this merge scenario is actually in scope if the expected output involves new indentation that should follow the fallback logic. So when the merged node is created, it inherits the first node's kind (paragraph) but adopts the second node's children. Since those children are already list-items, they don't need kind conversion—just re-indentation based on the paragraph's child base column, which is zero since paragraphs use their own indent level as the baseline for children.

The childBaseCol for the second list-item parent is 2 (its own indent of zero plus marker width), so the adopted children need to shift from column 2 down to column 0—a delta of -2. This is the core re-indentation mechanism mentioned in the code comments, but the problem emerges when that delta doesn't align with tab boundaries, causing the expected output to have children at column 0 with no leading whitespace. The issue is that `childBaseCol` is calculating the indentation shift using character width rather than actual tab width—it's subtracting the marker width (2 characters for "- ") instead of a full tab width (4 characters), which is why the children aren't dedenting by the correct amount when adopted into the paragraph.

content.thinking:
Looking at the `childBaseCol` function in reencode.ts, it determines the base column position for child elements by checking the parent type—returning 0 for root or heading nodes, adding the marker width for list items, or using the parent's indent level for paragraphs.

The issue is that this assumes CommonMark's strict convention where children align exactly under the marker, but real documents (especially tab-indented ones following Obsidian's convention) use one tab per nesting level regardless of marker width. This mismatch between the fixed formula and how actual documents are indented is the core problem.

Since the parser in parse.ts must already handle list continuation based on relative indentation rather than exact marker alignment to correctly parse tab-indented documents, I need to examine how parse.ts actually determines nesting relationships to understand what the correct child base column should be according to the document's real conventions.

I'm tracing through the list nesting logic—when a block's indent is less than the parent's content column, it pops from the stack, but when it's greater than or equal, it attaches as a child. The content column for each list item is calculated as the indent plus the marker length plus one, so for a dash marker that's indent plus 2.

The key insight is that this uses a "greater than or equal" comparison rather than exact alignment, which means children can be indented more than the strict marker-plus-one rule would suggest. A tab-indented child (indent 4) can still nest under a parent with content column 2, even though they're not exactly aligned. This matches CommonMark's actual behavior—children just need to be indented at least to the marker's content column, and extra indentation is allowed. But the shift subtree logic for calculating deltas assumes exact alignment, which creates a mismatch when children are more deeply indented than expected.

The real fix is to compute the actual indent width of the second's children from the document itself rather than deriving it from the markerWidth formula that assumes tight alignment. Calculating the actual child indent from the second node's first child instead of assuming it based on marker width, which gives us a delta of -4 for the repro case. When I apply this shift to the first child line, it removes exactly one tab character, leaving "- child1". The shiftSubtree function then recursively applies this same delta to all descendants, so grandchildren get shifted by the same amount. The approach handles edge cases well — if `second` has no children, the fallback formula still works fine since there's nothing to adopt anyway. For mixed indentation at the same depth, using the first child's actual indent width as the reference point is reasonable and aligns with how the codebase already samples from real document content elsewhere. Now I'm considering whether this approach is the right foundation for the fix. Measuring the actual indentation of existing children rather than computing an abstract marker width ensures the delta aligns perfectly to tab boundaries by construction. Since the user directed me to implement this fix on the current branch, I'll update the `mergeNodes` logic to calculate the shift based on real indentation values instead of the formula-based approach.

Updating merge logic... Refining the approach to measure the actual indentation of the target's existing children rather than relying on the formula, since adopted children will sit alongside first's own remaining children and need to match their actual indent convention. In the secondIsFirstChild case, I should look at `first.children[1]` if it exists to get the established column width for children, otherwise fall back to `first.children[0]`. This is getting complicated though, and the actual common case from the repro is when second isn't first's child at all — that's where the backspace merge happens.

Let me trace through the repro: the paragraph is at path [0], and its raw successor in document order is the list parent at [1], which is a top-level sibling, not a child. So secondParentPath is [] and firstParentPath is [], meaning secondIsFirstChild would be checking if [1] equals [0], which it doesn't.

Since they share the same parent (both top-level), we hit the sibling splice branch where the first node's children become the adopted children from the second node. The paragraph had no children of its own, so it ends up with the list parent's children as its only children.

The real issue is that the target side (paragraph) correctly uses its own indent column since paragraphs don't have marker-dependent indentation, but the source side (list item) has actual children indented differently than what the formula would assume. My fix of measuring the second node's actual child indent instead of relying on the formula handles this mismatch perfectly for this common scenario.

For the other case where the target is also a list item with existing tab-indented children, I should measure both sides' actual child indentation when available and fall back to the formula only when needed. I'm creating a helper function that checks if a node has children and uses their actual indent, otherwise defaults to the calculated base column. Applying the helper function in mergeNodes... When `secondIsFirstChild` is false, `first` must be childless since otherwise its successor would be its own first child, so the formula for `actualChildBaseCol(first)` works fine for that case. But when `secondIsFirstChild` is true, `first` already has children, and I need to make sure I'm using the correct base column for any additional children that existed before the merge happened. Writing helper function... Now I'm ready to implement this, and I can see that `indentWidth` is already imported from the parse module at the top of ops.ts, so I don't need to add any new imports.

content.thinking: I'm realizing the issue with my test assertion—I expected `merged.children` to have 2 items (child1 and child2), but it's actually returning 3 items, which means the children array is being flattened or merged differently than I thought. Let me trace through what's happening in the first test case where the paragraph absorbs the list parent.

So when the surgery result gets reparsed, the finalize function encodes and then parses it again, which changes how the structure gets interpreted. Since child1 moves to column 0 with no indentation, and "list parent2" (which was originally a separate top-level sibling after "list parent1") also ends up at column 0, the reparse causes "list parent2" to get absorbed as a child of the paragraph instead, because a list item at column 0 immediately following a paragraph gets nested under it during the reparse. The real issue is that when I re-encode and re-parse, the structural distinction between child1/child2 (which should be paragraph children) and list parent2 (which should remain a top-level sibling) gets lost—they all end up at the same indentation level with no separator, making them indistinguishable to the parser. I need to check whether this is a new bug I introduced or if the same problem exists in the original output. So the attachment rule that already exists throughout the codebase (where a list item directly following a paragraph becomes that paragraph's child despite zero relative indent) is actually the same mechanism at play here — the structural ambiguity isn't a bug in my fix, but rather an inherent limitation of how the encoding represents these relationships when everything lands at the same indentation level. Looking at the original markdown structure to verify how "list parent2" is parsed — since it's at indent 0, matching "list parent1"'s indent level, it should be recognized as a sibling at the same list level rather than nested under it. ent the actual structure in the original document to confirm whether the list is truly a top-level sibling or if the attachment rule makes it a child of the paragraph instead.

I was wrong about the structure—both list parents are actually children of the paragraph, with list-parent1 being the first child, which means my earlier analysis needs to be redone with the correct branch.

Now I'm recalculating the merged children and the indent shift: the reference child from the paragraph is list-parent2 (with 0 indent), while the reference child from list-parent1 is child1 (with 4 spaces of indent), giving a childShift of -4. This shift removes exactly one tab from the nested items, so child1 and child2 become top-level with no indent, while grandchild1 retains one tab of indentation.

The result is that the paragraph now has three direct children at the top level: child1 (with grandchild1 nested beneath it), child2, and list-parent2 — all at zero indent. This matches what the test output showed, but I'm questioning whether this is actually the correct behavior or a bug in the attachment logic. When list-parent1 gets absorbed into the paragraph, its children (child1 and child2) are promoted to become direct children of the paragraph, taking the position where list-parent1 was, while list-parent2 stays as the next sibling — resulting in exactly three children at the paragraph level, which matches what my fix produces.

The real issue is that my test assertion was wrong. I expected only two children (child1 and child2) in the merged node, but I forgot that list-parent2 was already a child of the paragraph before the merge and remains there afterward, so the actual result is correct. The test assertions need to be corrected to account for the three actual children in the merged list (child1, child2, and the pre-existing list parent2) rather than the two I mistakenly expected.

## 1. Investigate

- [x] 1.1 Confirm the root cause: `ops.ts`'s `inferIndentUnit` hardcodes `'  '` as its
      final fallback, reached whenever a structural op materializes brand-new
      indentation with no existing evidence in the document.

      public API. Confirmed `Vault.getConfig`/`setConfig` are NOT in the public
      `obsidian.d.ts` surface (violates this project's "100% public API" bar).
- [x] 1.3 Empirically verify, against a real Obsidian instance (via the e2e harness's
      `executeObsidian`), that CM6's public `@codemirror/language` `indentUnit` facet
      tracks the "Indent using tabs" setting live — `useTab: false` → space string
      (width = `tabSize`), `useTab: true` → `"\t"`.

## 2. Thread the fallback through ops.ts / enforce.ts

- [x] 2.1 `ops.ts`: `inferIndentUnit(doc, fallback = '  ')`, `destinationIndent(doc,
      parent, siblings, fallbackIndentUnit?)`.
- [x] 2.2 Thread `fallbackIndentUnit?` through `indent`, `outdent`, `splitNode`,
      `reencodeBlocksForDestination`, `insertSubtrees`.
- [x] 2.3 `enforce.ts`: thread `fallbackIndentUnit` through `computeVerdict`,

content.content:
1	## Why
2	
3	Outline mode always materializes brand-new list indentation as two literal spaces,
4	ignoring Obsidian's own "Indent using tabs" editor setting (Settings → Editor). Any
5	vault configured for tabs (or a different space width) gets mixed indentation the
6	moment the outliner creates a fresh indent level with nothing in the document yet to
7	infer a unit from — the first Tab in a new note, the first structural paste into an
8	otherwise-flat scope, an Enter-driven split that materializes a new nested child.
9	Since `structural-operations`' own "Context-determined encoding on reparent" rule
10	already infers the unit from EXISTING document content when there is any, this only
11	bites the "nothing to infer from yet" case — but that case is common (every new note,
12	every first indent) and the mismatch it produces is exactly the kind of "breaks
13	indentation in multiple cases" a user notices immediately.
14	
15	## What Changes
16	
17	- `ops.ts`'s `inferIndentUnit`/`destinationIndent` gain an optional
18	  `fallbackIndentUnit` parameter, used only when the document itself has no existing
19	  indented list item to infer a unit from (unchanged default: two spaces, so every
20	  existing pure-function caller/test is unaffected).
21	- This threads through `indent`, `outdent`, `splitNode`, `reencodeBlocksForDestination`,
22	  `insertSubtrees` (ops.ts) and `computeVerdict` and its internal helpers (enforce.ts),
23	  covering every place brand-new indentation can be materialized: Tab/Shift-Tab,
24	  Enter-driven splits, and structural paste/type-over/merge-splice rewrites.
25	- The CM6 adapters (`keymap.ts` for Tab/Shift-Tab, `transaction-filter.ts` for the
26	  paste/type-over verdict path) supply the live value by reading the PUBLIC
27	  `@codemirror/language` `indentUnit` facet off the editor state — confirmed live
28	  against a real Obsidian instance that this facet tracks Obsidian's "Indent using
29	  tabs" setting exactly, with no private `vault.getConfig`/`vault.setConfig` API
30	  needed in the shipped plugin code (this project's architecture bar: 100% public
31	  Obsidian APIs).
32	- **Known gap, not fixed here**: `main.ts`'s command-palette structural commands
33	  (`indent-node`/`outdent-node`) have no public-API path to the live CM6 facet from
34	  Obsidian's `Editor`/`MarkdownView` types, so they keep inferring from document
35	  content only (same as before this change) — documented inline at the `StructuralOp`
36	  type. This is a narrow, secondary entry point; Tab/Shift-Tab and paste (the
37	  everyday paths) are fully fixed.
38	- New devDependency: `@codemirror/language` (types only — already externalized in
39	  `esbuild.config.mjs`, resolved to Obsidian's own bundled copy at runtime, so no
40	  extra bytes ship and the facet reference is the SAME instance Obsidian itself sets).
41	
42	## Capabilities
43	
44	### New Capabilities
45	(none)
46	
47	### Modified Capabilities
48	- `structural-operations`: brand-new indentation (no existing document evidence to
49	  infer a unit from) now takes the vault's "Indent using tabs" preference instead of
50	  a hardcoded two-space default.
51	
52	## Impact
53	
54	- `src/ops.ts`, `src/enforce.ts`: optional trailing parameter threaded through the
55	  functions listed above; no behavior change for any existing caller that omits it.
56	- `src/plugin/keymap.ts`, `src/plugin/transaction-filter.ts`: read the public CM6
57	  `indentUnit` facet and pass it through.
58	- `src/plugin/main.ts`: doc comment only, no behavior change (documents the known
59	  command-palette gap).
60	- `src/plugin/grammar.ts`: `planKey` gains the same optional parameter.
61	- `package.json`/`package-lock.json`: `@codemirror/language` devDependency added.
62	- Tests: new unit coverage in `tests/ops.test.ts` (fallback-unit behavior for
63	  `indent`/`splitNode`), a new e2e test in `e2e/specs/30-keyboard-grammar.e2e.ts`
64	  toggling the real Obsidian setting and asserting Tab output, and a new
65	  test-setup-only `setIndentUsingTabs` helper in `e2e/helpers.ts`.
66

file.content:
## Why

Outline mode always materializes brand-new list indentation as two literal spaces,
ignoring Obsidian's own "Indent using tabs" editor setting (Settings → Editor). Any
vault configured for tabs (or a different space width) gets mixed indentation the
moment the outliner creates a fresh indent level with nothing in the document yet to
infer a unit from — the first Tab in a new note, the first structural paste into an
otherwise-flat scope, an Enter-driven split that materializes a new nested child.
Since `structural-operations`' own "Context-determined encoding on reparent" rule
already infers the unit from EXISTING document content when there is any, this only
bites the "nothing to infer from yet" case — but that case is common (every new note,
every first indent) and the mismatch it produces is exactly the kind of "breaks
indentation in multiple cases" a user notices immediately.

## What Changes

- `ops.ts`'s `inferIndentUnit`/`destinationIndent` gain an optional
  `fallbackIndentUnit` parameter, used only when the document itself has no existing
  indented list item to infer a unit from (unchanged default: two spaces, so every
  existing pure-function caller/test is unaffected).
- This threads through `indent`, `outdent`, `splitNode`, `reencodeBlocksForDestination`,
  `insertSubtrees` (ops.ts) and `computeVerdict` and its internal helpers (enforce.ts),
  covering every place brand-new indentation can be materialized: Tab/Shift-Tab,
  Enter-driven splits, and structural paste/type-over/merge-splice rewrites.
- The CM6 adapters (`keymap.ts` for Tab/Shift-Tab, `transaction-filter.ts` for the
  paste/type-over verdict path) supply the live value by reading the PUBLIC
  `@codemirror/language` `indentUnit` facet off the editor state — confirmed live
  against a real Obsidian instance that this facet tracks Obsidian's "Indent using
  tabs" setting exactly, with no private `vault.getConfig`/`vault.setConfig` API
  needed in the shipped plugin code (this project's architecture bar: 100% public
  Obsidian APIs).
- **Known gap, not fixed here**: `main.ts`'s command-palette structural commands
  (`indent-node`/`outdent-node`) have no public-API path to the live CM6 facet from
  Obsidian's `Editor`/`MarkdownView` types, so they keep inferring from document
  content only (same as before this change) — documented inline at the `StructuralOp`

  everyday paths) are fully fixed.

  `esbuild.config.mjs`, resolved to Obsidian's own bundled copy at runtime, so no
  extra bytes ship and the facet reference is the SAME instance Obsidian itself sets).

  infer a unit from) now takes the vault's "Indent using tabs" preference instead of

input.new_string:
- New devDependency: `@codemirror/language` (types only — already externalized in
  `esbuild.config.mjs`, resolved to Obsidian's own bundled copy at runtime, so no
  extra bytes ship and the facet reference is the SAME instance Obsidian itself sets).

  bug (reproduces identically on `main`, unaffected by the fallback-unit work above) —
  absorbing a node re-parents its children via a flat numeric COLUMN-width delta
  (`childBaseCol(first) - childBaseCol(second)`) computed from an ASSUMED
  marker-alignment formula, not the children's actual existing indentation. Tab-indented
  documents commonly indent children a full tab past the marker rather than exactly
  `markerWidth` columns past it (the parser already accepts this — nesting only
  requires indent ≥ the marker's content column, not exact alignment); when the
  assumed delta doesn't land on a whole tab boundary, the dedent leaves a fractional
  remainder that gets padded with SPACES mid-tab, corrupting a pure-tab subtree into
  mixed indentation (and breaking the innermost node's own list-item parse). Fixed by
  measuring the shift against each side's ACTUAL existing child indentation (sampled
  from a real surviving sibling child, when one exists) instead of the assumed
  formula — same theme as the rest of this change (respect what the document/vault
  actually uses over a hardcoded assumption), so it stays in this change rather than
  becoming a separate one.
